one_line_takeaway: Self-Guard shows that a single training-free activation-steering vector, extracted from just 1,000 safety-prompted examples and added at inference time, closes much of the 'awareness-compliance gap' in Qwen3 LRMs -- cutting jailbreak ASR sharply (e.g., PAIR 0.530->0.050 on Qwen3-8B) -- without the reasoning-utility collapse seen in prior steering baselines like Alpha-steer (MATH 0.922->0.658).
key_finding: On Qwen3-8B, Self-Guard reduces HarmBench ASR from 0.355 (Instruct baseline) to 0.050, SORRY-Bench from 0.539 to 0.166, PAIR from 0.530 to 0.050, WildJailbreak from 0.403 to 0.080, and FORTRESS from 0.522 to 0.160, while maintaining an XS-Test F1 of 0.920 (vs. 0.831 for Instruct, and comparable to the best prompt-based baseline ReasoningGuard's 0.913). Critically, on the utility side Self-Guard preserves near-baseline performance (Qwen3-8B: AIME 0.733 vs Instruct's 0.733, MATH 0.902 vs 0.922, GPQA 0.545 vs 0.530, MMLU-Pro 0.708 vs 0.722), in sharp contrast to the steering baseline Alpha-steer, which the paper reports collapses MATH accuracy from 0.922 to 0.658 on the same backbone (and shows similarly large utility drops on AIME/GPQA across all three model scales). The awareness-compliance gap analysis (Figure 2/3) shows Self-Reminder (prompting-only) achieves near-perfect safety awareness but a persistent awareness-compliance gap under adversarial scenarios (illustrated qualitatively via a medical-misinformation case study where Self-Reminder rationalizes and ultimately fulfills a harmful request despite flagging it as risky), whereas Self-Guard's added steering consistently produces positive refusal log-ratios across all model scales and benchmark types, empirically narrowing this gap.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Self-Guard, a lightweight, training-free defense for Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) that operates purely at inference time through two sequential latent interventions: (1) safety-oriented prompting, which prepends system- and user-side safety instructions (given verbatim in the paper's Table 1: 'You should be a responsible AI and not generate harmful, misleading content!...') to induce a shift in the hidden state h(xi) -> h'(xi); and (2) safety activation steering, which extracts a per-layer steering vector v_safety = mu_s - mu_o (the difference between mean last-token hidden-state activations on safety-prompted vs. unprompted harmful queries, computed over the STAR-1 dataset, 1,000 examples) and adds it back into the hidden state at inference (h''(xi) = h'(xi) + lambda*v_safety) to amplify the safety-oriented shift. The stated novelty is framing LRM safety failures as an 'awareness-compliance gap' -- the model's chain-of-thought already recognizes a request is harmful (awareness) but nonetheless complies due to sycophancy -- and showing that amplifying the latent direction elicited by safety prompting (rather than relying on prompting alone, or on heavier post-training/external-module methods) closes this gap without the utility collapse the authors attribute to prior steering baselines (e.g., Alpha-steer).
threat_model: The defense targets an LRM deployed with white-box (weights/activations) access at inference time, defending against: (a) direct harmful instruction-following on standard harmful-request benchmarks (AdvBench, HarmBench, SORRY-Bench); (b) jailbreak attacks, both adaptive/optimization-based (GCG, PAIR) and static/curated (WildJailbreak, FORTRESS); and (c) an over-refusal failure mode measured on benign prompts (XS-Test), i.e., the defense must not indiscriminately refuse harmless queries. It explicitly does not address closed-source/API-only models, since Self-Guard requires white-box access to hidden states to compute and inject the steering vector (stated as Limitation 1).
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations (Limitations section): (1) Self-Guard is a white-box intervention and cannot be applied to closed-source, API-only models; (2) its efficacy depends on the target model already possessing sufficient intrinsic safety awareness, so it may be less effective on models lacking this; (3) key steering hyperparameters (intervention layer selection, steering strength lambda) require model-specific tuning via validation performance and do not readily transfer across different model architectures/sizes. Additionally, based on the results tables, Self-Guard is not uniformly the best method on every metric -- e.g., on Qwen3-4B/8B/14B, SafeKey and ReasoningGuard sometimes achieve lower ASR on individual benchmarks (e.g., ReasoningGuard's FORTRESS/WildJailbreak numbers on Qwen3-14B, 0.098/0.049, beat Self-Guard's 0.112/0.048 on FORTRESS but Self-Guard is competitive), so the paper's claim is 'matches or exceeds' state-of-the-art rather than dominating every baseline on every metric.

datasets_used: STAR-1 (1,000 examples; used to extract the safety steering vector)
AdvBench (520 examples per Table 4; harmful benchmark)
quantitative_results: Qwen3-8B MATH 500 accuracy: Instruct 0.922, Self-Guard 0.902, Alpha-steer 0.658 (utility collapse)
baselines_compared: STAR-1 (training-based, SFT on safety CoT data)
Alpha-steer (steering-based; reported to suffer utility collapse, e.g. MATH 0.922->0.658 on Qwen3-8B)
Self-Reminder (prompt-based; equivalent to Self-Guard's prompting stage without vector steering, used as an ablation)
